content.full_text:
Fern Por.rrrcer- PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0986
4pri120,2007
This letter is in response to your request for written advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest and gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act').r "g""u"L iernents of your
conflict of interest question concern past conduct, we are declining to provide advice ,"giaiig
this issue' (Regulation 18329(b)(8XA).) Therefore, nothing in this letter evaluates any previous
conduct and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. our advice is
based on the facts you provide. The Fair Political Practiles Commission (..Commission,) does
not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re oglesby (1975) i fppc ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Do fishing trips taken on your friend's fishing boat constitute a reportable gift under the
Act where you buy lunches, bait, and pay for half of the gas to take the trip?
Roy Parmentier
136 N Star Street
Lakeport, Califomia 9 5 453 -4 537
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A.-07-028
Dear Mr. Parmentier:
I Govemment Code Sectiors 81000 - 91014
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
CONCLUSIONS
Commission Regulations appear at Title 2, Sections lgl09_
Generally, when an official receives something of value and provides consideration of
equal or greater value in exchange, there is no gift rrrd". the Act. The lunches, bait, and
payments for gas you provided on the day of each fishing trip are consideration provided toward
the value of the fishing trip. However, we do not have sufficient information to determine
whether the value of this consideration was equal to or greater than the value of the fishing trip.
If it was of equal or greater value, there is no gift to youl If it was less than eq;al value, the
difference between the value of the fishing trip and tire value of the consideration you provided is
a gift to you subject to the $50 reporting requiiement and the $390 gift limit.
a=


FileNo. A-07-028
Page No. 2
You are the mayor of the City of Lakeport. Jim Bums has been an acquaintance of yours
for over 15 years- You are an avid fisherman and ran into Jim Burns while on a fishing trip in
Fort Bragg one to two years ago. Mr. Burns asked if you would like to accompanytrim on trls
fishing boat so that you both could fish together. In the past year, you have fished with
Mr. Burns atotal of six times. on each of these kips you bought bait, lunches, and on one
occasion gave Mr' Burns a filet tray for his boat. You also shared expenses for gas. you have
also allowed Mr. Burns to use your fishing boat when he was vacationing in McCloud.
In the past yea4 Mr. Burns has been representing a group of investors who are tryrng to
determine if it would be feasible to build a new golf course and housing development in
Lakeport. In our telephone conversation you mentioned that you have-alreadyparticipated in
govemmental decisions related to this development project.
FACTS
ANALYSIS
Gfts - Generally
Section 87203 provides that "[e]veryperson who holds an office specified in Section
87200 shall . . - file a statement disclosing his [or her] inveshnents, his loitrerl interests in real
property and his [or her] income . . .." _In99me includes gifts. (Section SZO:O) As a city
councilmember, you are a person who holds an office sp-cified in Section g7200.
Additionally, Section 89503(a) provides that no elected officer of a local government
agency shall accept gifts from any single source in any calendar year worth in exiess of the gift
Iimit. The current gift limit is $390. (Regulationlg94O.2.)
Section 87207 provides that gifts of $50 or more in value must be reported. (Section
87207(a)(t).)
Section 82028(a) defines "*O" as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is noi received . . ..,,
The Neighborlines s Exception
The Commission has previously indicated that certain tlpes of benefits are not gifts, but
instead "acts of neighborliness." (In re cory e975) 1 Fppc ops. rs:.; rn cory,the
Commission was asked whether an official had received a gift fron u neighbor who had assisted
him in repairing his_fence. In providin gthat such servic"r *o" "acts of rieighborliness,, instead
of reportable gifts, the Commission stated that is was "absurd to suppose ttralt the repairing of a
fence by a neighbor, the offering of a ride, the fixing of a flat tire, or hundreds of similarli
friendly acts are 'grfts' which must be reported under the Act . . ..,,


File No. A-07-028
Page No. 3
The Commission later offered certain factors that"arerelevant to determining whether a
service provided to a public official is a nonreportable act of neighborliness or a gift. fhe
Commission stated that among the factors to be considered is '\rhether the donoihas, or in the
foreseeable future mayhave business before the official who receives the seryice.,, The
Commission stated:
"While the absence of this factor does not necessarilymean that no
gift has been made, the presence of this factor will in most cases
provide strong, if not conclusive, evidence that a gift has been
made. rtmay be that the donor has no intent of attempting to
influence the official, but the need to avoid even the appearance of
possible impropriety is reason alone to require that a service
provided to an official under these circumstances be disclosed.,,
(In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 5a.)
Because Mr- Burns has been or will be appearing before you, and the fishing trips were
taken close to the time he began appearing before you, we do noi believe the ..acts of
neighborliness" exception applies. Accordingly, the fishing trips you took with Mr. Burns
constitute 'oglfts" under the Act to the extent that the benefit you received was greater than what
you paid in consideration for each trip.
Return, Donation, or Reimbursement of a Gift
Section 82028 and Regulation 18943 provide for the return, donation, or
reimbursement of a gift. Regulation rc9a3@)$) states that agift is not considered accepted or
received if;
"The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, reimburses
the donor, or the donor's agent or intermediary, for ail or a portion
of the gift. In such event the value of the gift is reduced bythe
amount of the reimbursement, and the amount of the any gift or
activity expense which must be disclosed is reduced by the amount
of the reimbursement."
Cash Payments: So long as the cash consideration you provided to Mr. Burns for the
trips (which he may have used for bait, gas, food, etc.) was paid within 30 days, and assuming
the payment was equal to or greater in value than the fishing trip, no gift wasieceived.
(Regulation I 89 a3 @)@).)
Noncash Payments: In addition, non-cash consideration exchanged during the same
outing, (and not at some later date) for which the consideration given was of equal or greater
value than the benefit received would not result in a gift.


File No. A-07-028
Page No. 4
Because you have not provided facts to determine the value of what you received in
exchange for the consideration you provided on these fishing trips, we are unable to determine
whether or not the consideration you provided is equal to or greater than the value of the benefit
received' However' to guide you in making this determinatiJn, Regulation tti+aqlprovides:
"whenever the fair market varue cannot readily be ascertained
because the gift is unique or unusual, the value shall be the cost to
the donor, if known or ascertainable. If the cost to the donor is
unknown and unascertainable, the recipient shall make a
reasonable approximation. In making such an approximation, the
recipient shall take into account the price of similar iterns. If
similar items are not available as a guide, a good faith estimate
shall be utilized,j'
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
SincerelS
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Le gal Divi sion
)htuk-e,a^-
SKBjsl
I:\Adviceltrs\07-028
